does: This paper presents an empirical survey of 3,270 UK adults conducted in 2024 to characterize how everyday users of conversational agents like ChatGPT and Gemini engage in behaviors that could introduce security and privacy risks. The study identifies that approximately one-third of the surveyed population uses CA services regularly, and among these regular users, up to one-third exhibit behaviors potentially enabling attacks while one-quarter have attempted jailbreaking. The researchers document user practices around data sanitization, sensitivity awareness, and information sharing patterns, revealing that while most participants report not sharing sensitive data and roughly half sanitize their inputs, a significant portion engage in jailbreak attempts driven by curiosity, entertainment, or information-seeking motivations. The findings quantify the prevalence of risky user behaviors across a representative population sample.
connects: This work addresses a critical gap between known technical vulnerabilities in large language models—including jailbreaks, prompt injection, and code execution exploits documented in recent security research—and actual real-world user exposure to these risks. It builds on prior work characterizing LLM vulnerabilities and adversarial attacks by grounding these technical threats in user behavior, connecting to human-computer security research examining how ordinary users interact with systems in ways that inadvertently create exploitable conditions. The study extends beyond controlled laboratory settings to measure actual prevalence of risky behaviors in representative populations, complementing prior work on user awareness of AI risks and security practices in other domains.
enables: These findings establish a foundation for designing user-centered security interventions and educating conversational agent users about behavioral risks. The prevalence data enables future work on developing better warnings, interface designs, and safety guardrails tailored to actual user populations and risk profiles. The results motivate research into how to communicate security concerns effectively to non-technical users, inform policy decisions around responsible AI deployment, and guide the prioritization of technical mitigations for the most commonly exploited user behaviors. The work also sets a benchmark for longitudinal studies tracking how user risk awareness and practices evolve as CA technology and public knowledge about vulnerabilities mature.
